nodes.name: Alphabets
nodes.description: Phonetic alphabets, classical alphabets, and letter systems

config.prompt: Name all 26 words of the NATO phonetic alphabet in order (A through Z)

items.explanation:
Represents the letter A
Spelled 'Alfa' in the official NATO/ICAO standard to avoid confusion in languages where 'ph' is not pronounced as 'f'
Adopted in its current form in 1956 after extensive international testing
Also the first letter of the Greek alphabet

Represents the letter B
Also an Italian/Spanish exclamation of approval, traditionally shouted at opera performances
In military usage, 'Bravo' can designate a second element (Alpha team, Bravo team, etc.)
Replaced 'Baker' from the earlier Joint Army/Navy Phonetic Alphabet (JANAP)

Represents the letter C
In Vietnam War slang, 'Charlie' referred to the Viet Cong (from 'Victor Charlie,' the NATO phonetic for VC)
Retained from the Joint Army/Navy Phonetic Alphabet (JANAP), which unified the earlier Navy 'Cast' and Army 'Charlie' under 'Charlie' — one of the few words retained
In British slang, 'a right Charlie' means a fool

Represents the letter D
Shared with the 4th letter of the Greek alphabet (Δ/δ)
In geography, a river delta is named for the triangular shape of the Greek letter
Delta Air Lines (founded 1924) is named after the Mississippi River Delta region

Represents the letter E
In Greek mythology, Echo was a nymph cursed by Hera to only repeat the last words spoken to her
In audio engineering, echo is a distinct repetition of sound caused by reflection off a surface
Amazon named its smart speaker 'Echo' (2014), using the wake word 'Alexa'

Represents the letter F
Also a ballroom dance in 4/4 time, originating around 1914 and attributed to vaudeville performer Harry Fox
One of the standard dances in competitive ballroom dancing
Replaced 'Fox' from the Joint Army/Navy Phonetic Alphabet

Represents the letter G
The sport of golf originated in 15th-century Scotland; the word may derive from Scots gouf ('to strike')
The folk etymology that 'golf' stands for 'Gentlemen Only, Ladies Forbidden' is false
Replaced 'George' from the Joint Army/Navy Phonetic Alphabet

Represents the letter H
From French hôtel, itself from Old French hostel (which also gives us 'hostel')
Chosen for international recognizability, as the word is used in most European languages
Replaced 'How' from the Joint Army/Navy Phonetic Alphabet

Represents the letter I
The country name derives from the Indus River, from Sanskrit sindhu ('river, sea')
Replaced 'Item' from the Joint Army/Navy Phonetic Alphabet
Not to be confused with the NATO country code for India (IND)

Represents the letter J
The official ICAO spelling is 'Juliett' (with double-t) to indicate the final syllable is pronounced
Named evocative of Shakespeare's Juliet from Romeo and Juliet (c. 1597)
Replaced 'Jig' from the Joint Army/Navy Phonetic Alphabet

Represents the letter K
Also the SI prefix for 1,000, from Greek chilioi ('thousand')
In informal usage, 'kilo' is short for kilogram
Replaced 'King' from the Joint Army/Navy Phonetic Alphabet

Represents the letter L
Shares its name with the capital of Peru, founded by Francisco Pizarro in 1535
Pronounced 'LEE-mah' in NATO usage, matching the city's Spanish pronunciation
Replaced 'Love' from the Joint Army/Navy Phonetic Alphabet

Represents the letter M
In military communications, 'mike' also informally means 'minute' ('wait one mike')
Retained from the Joint Army/Navy Phonetic Alphabet
One of the most intuitive NATO codewords, as Mike is a common English name

Represents the letter N
From Latin novem ('nine') — November was the 9th month in the original Roman calendar before January and February were added
Replaced 'Nan' from the Joint Army/Navy Phonetic Alphabet
The longest NATO phonetic word at 8 letters

Represents the letter O
In maritime signal flags, 'Oscar' means 'man overboard'
Shares its name with the Academy Awards nickname; the origin of 'Oscar' for the statuette is disputed
Replaced 'Oboe' from the Joint Army/Navy Phonetic Alphabet

Represents the letter P
Chosen for universal recognizability — 'papa' means 'father' in many languages worldwide
Replaced 'Peter' from the Joint Army/Navy Phonetic Alphabet
Pronounced 'pah-PAH' with stress on the second syllable in NATO usage

Represents the letter Q
Named after the Canadian province; the word is from Algonquin kébec ('where the river narrows')
Replaced 'Queen' from the Joint Army/Navy Phonetic Alphabet
One of the few NATO phonetic words derived from a place name in the Americas

Represents the letter R
Evokes Shakespeare's Romeo from Romeo and Juliet — paired with Juliet (J) in the same alphabet
Replaced 'Roger' from the Joint Army/Navy Phonetic Alphabet; 'Roger' was reassigned to mean 'received/understood'
In Italian, romeo means 'pilgrim to Rome'

Represents the letter S
From Spanish sierra ('mountain range'), originally from Latin serra ('saw') for the jagged peaks
Replaced 'Sugar' from the Joint Army/Navy Phonetic Alphabet
Shared with the Sierra Nevada ranges in both California and Spain

Represents the letter T
Also the Argentine partner dance originating in Buenos Aires in the late 19th century
In military slang, 'tango' can mean 'target' or 'enemy'
Replaced 'Tare' from the Joint Army/Navy Phonetic Alphabet

Represents the letter U
From Latin uniformis ('having one form') — fitting for a military alphabet standardizing communication
Replaced 'Uncle' from the Joint Army/Navy Phonetic Alphabet
The NATO phonetic alphabet itself is a kind of uniform standard across all allied militaries

Represents the letter V
'Victor Charlie' (V-C) became Vietnam War slang for the Viet Cong
Retained from the Joint Army/Navy Phonetic Alphabet — one of the few words kept unchanged in the NATO version
From Latin victor ('conqueror'), from vincere ('to conquer')

Represents the letter W
From Irish Gaelic uisce beatha or Scottish Gaelic uisge beatha ('water of life')
The official ICAO spelling is 'Whiskey' (with an 'e'), following the Irish/American convention rather than Scottish 'whisky'
Replaced 'William' from the Joint Army/Navy Phonetic Alphabet

Represents the letter X
X-rays were discovered by Wilhelm Röntgen in 1895; he called them 'X' because the radiation was unknown
Retained from the Joint Army/Navy Phonetic Alphabet, where it also represented X
One of the few NATO words written with a hyphen

Represents the letter Y
Origin of 'Yankee' is debated: possibly from Dutch Janke (diminutive of Jan) or a Native American pronunciation of 'English'
In the American Revolution, British soldiers used 'Yankee' as a pejorative; Americans reclaimed it with 'Yankee Doodle'
Replaced 'Yoke' from the Joint Army/Navy Phonetic Alphabet

Represents the letter Z
Also the name of the Bantu ethnic group in South Africa and their language (isiZulu)
'Zulu time' is military/aviation slang for UTC (Coordinated Universal Time), because Z = Zulu
Replaced 'Zebra' from the Joint Army/Navy Phonetic Alphabet
